summary: Charity Majors describes Honeycomb's experiment of discussing hypothetical ethical dilemmas monthly as a team. She argues that ethical problems are fractally hard and that practicing these discussions when stakes are low helps teams make better decisions under pressure, builds trust, and amplifies employee voices.
key_points: Ethical problems appear simple on the surface but reveal fractal complexity the closer you examine them
Honeycomb holds monthly company-wide discussions of hypothetical ethical scenarios
Practicing difficult decisions in low-stakes settings prepares teams for real crises
Transparency and employee voice-amplification build trust for when fast decisions are needed
Companies aren't democracies but leaders have a responsibility to understand where their team stands
money_quotes: Ethical problems are hard. Even the ones that seem straightforward on the face of them get stickier the closer you look at them. There are more stakeholders, more caveats, more cautionary tales, more unintended consequences than you can generally see at face value. It's like fractal hardness, and anyone who thinks it's easy is fooling themselves.

People make the worst possible decisions when they're taken off guard, when they are in an unfamiliar situation (and often panicking). Talking through a bunch of nightmare scenarios is a way for us to exercise these decision-making muscles while the stakes are low.

We all get to experience what it's like to hear a problem, have a kneejerk reaction .. then peeling back the onion to reveal layer after layer of dismaying complexities that muddy our snap certainties.
We believe that companies are created every day by the people who show up to labor together, so those people have a right to know most things.
key_insight: Regularly practicing ethical decision-making as a team when stakes are low builds the trust and judgment needed to navigate real crises well.
